coredata.dc:title: Application of decorated magnetic nanophotocatalysts for efficient photodegradation of organic dye: A comparison study on photocatalytic activity of magnetic zinc sulfide and graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Karimi, Hadi
Rajabi, Hamid Reza
Kavoshi, Leila
coredata.dc:description: In the present research, two magnetic nanophotocatalysts (MNPCs) including iron oxide nanoparticles decorated with zinc sulfide quantum dots (Fe3O4@ZnS QDs), and N,S- doped graphene QDs (Fe3O4 @ N, S-GQDs) were synthesized by the sonochemical and hydrothermal methods, respectively. The prepared MNPCs were characterized by various techniques including X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM), UV–vis absorption and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. The SEM images show the formation of nano- sized particles of Fe3O4, also the morphology and size of the particles were changed after their decoration with ZnS and GQDs. The magnetic saturation values of Fe3O4@ZnS QDs and Fe3O4 @ N, S-GQDs samples were obtained as 19.43 and 55.58 emu.g−1, respectively. The desirable paramagnetic properties of the samples provide suitable magnetic separation process and simple recyclyig of MNPCs. The photocatalytic activity of the as-synthesized samples was evaluated through investigating their ability to degrade an organic pollutant (i.e. Victoria Blue R dye) in aqueous media. The photodegradation efficiency was evaluated based on the decrease of VBR concentration at its maximum absorption wavelength, in the presence of Fe3O4@ZnS QDs and Fe3O4 @ N, S-GQDs as nanophotocatalyst and under ultraviolet (UV) and visible (Vis) light irradiations, respectively. In optimization experiments, the effect of various factors including pH, dye concentration, irradiation time, MNPC dosage, type and concentration of salts were investigated. The maximum photodegradation percentage of Fe3O4@ZnS QDs was obtained as 93.44 % at pH 8, and in the case of Fe3O4 @ N, S-GQDs as 96.68 % at pH 9, after 120 min irradiation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Decorated magnetic nanophotocatalyst
Victoria Blue R dye
Zinc sulfide
Doped graphene quantum dots

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of unfunctionalized and functionalized Fe3O4 and Fe3O4 decorated with ZnS QDs
Synthesis of N,S-GQDs and Fe3O4 decorated with N,S-GQDs
Photodegradation of VBR using the decorated MNPCs
Optical properties of the as-prepared samples
The effect of initial dye concentration and irradiation time
Reusability of Fe3O4@ZnS QDs and Fe3O4@ N, S-GQDs MNPCs
The effect of salt, co-existing ions, and enhancers on VBR dye degradation
Conflict of interest and authorship conformation form

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (A) Fe3O4, (B) Fe3O4 functionalized with SDS, (C) Fe3O4@ ZnS, and (D) Fe3O4@N, S-GQDs samples.
UV–vis absorption and FT-IR spectra of the samples: (A, B) Fe3O4@ ZnS and (C,D) Fe3O4@N, S-GQDs.
VSM patterns of the as-synthesized samples.
SEM images of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@ SDS, Fe3O4@ ZnS and Fe3O4@N, S-GQDs samples with different magnifications.
(A,B) The results of zero charge point experiments and (C,D) the effect of pH on the photodegradation efficiency of VBR dye in the presence of MNPCs.
The effect of ionic strength, foreign ions and enhancing agents on degradation efficiency of Fe3O4@ZnS QDs.
Sonochemical synthetic representation of Fe3O4 decorated with ZnSQDs.
Sonochemical synthetic representation of N,S-GQDs and Fe3O4 decorated with N,S-GQDs by hydrothermal and sonochemical methods.
The schematic representation of photodegradation of VBR using the as-prepred MNPCs.
The comparison between the characteristics of the nanophotocatalysts used in the present research.
Comparison the performance features of the present nanophotocatalysts with some previously reported ones for removal of VBR dye.

head.ce:title: Application of decorated magnetic nanophotocatalysts for efficient photodegradation of organic dye: A comparison study on photocatalytic activity of magnetic zinc sulfide and graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Yasouj University, Yasouj, 75918-74831, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: a Chemistry Department, Yasouj University, Yasouj, 75918-74831, Iran.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Engineering Department, Naghshe Jahan Institute of Higher Education, Baharestan, Isfahan, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Naghshe Jahan Institute of Higher Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: b Chemical Engineering Department, Naghshe Jahan Institute of Higher Education, Baharestan, Isfahan, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present research, two magnetic nanophotocatalysts (MNPCs) including iron oxide nanoparticles decorated with zinc sulfide quantum dots (Fe3O4@ZnS QDs), and N,S- doped graphene QDs (Fe3O4 @ N, S-GQDs) were synthesized by the sonochemical and hydrothermal methods, respectively. The prepared MNPCs were characterized by various techniques including X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM), UV–vis absorption and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. The SEM images show the formation of nano- sized particles of Fe3O4, also the morphology and size of the particles were changed after their decoration with ZnS and GQDs. The magnetic saturation values of Fe3O4@ZnS QDs and Fe3O4 @ N, S-GQDs samples were obtained as 19.43 and 55.58 emu.g−1, respectively. The desirable paramagnetic properties of the samples provide suitable magnetic separation process and simple recyclyig of MNPCs. The photocatalytic activity of the as-synthesized samples was evaluated through investigating their ability to degrade an organic pollutant (i.e. Victoria Blue R dye) in aqueous media. The photodegradation efficiency was evaluated based on the decrease of VBR concentration at its maximum absorption wavelength, in the presence of Fe3O4@ZnS QDs and Fe3O4 @ N, S-GQDs as nanophotocatalyst and under ultraviolet (UV) and visible (Vis) light irradiations, respectively. In optimization experiments, the effect of various factors including pH, dye concentration, irradiation time, MNPC dosage, type and concentration of salts were investigated. The maximum photodegradation percentage of Fe3O4@ZnS QDs was obtained as 93.44 % at pH 8, and in the case of Fe3O4 @ N, S-GQDs as 96.68 % at pH 9, after 120 min irradiation.

ce:section.ce:para: Considering the industrial growth, the increase of organic pollutants has created massive crises in human societies. Pollution caused by synthetic dyes is a global concern. For example, the azo dyes, account for 50 % of the current dye market share [1]. Because of their persistence that leads to serious health and environmental risks for humans, removal of the pollutant dyes from wastewater is extremely important [2]. Most dyes have one or more benzene rings which are such toxic and hard to decompose, that if not be treated, can cause irreparable damages to the environment [3]. Many of these dyes are resistant to biodegradation processes [4]. So, the remained dye in the sewage of dyeing chamber, cause serious problems for the environment through increasing the chemical oxygen demand (COD) and preventing the sunlight penetration into the depth of water, and hence, disruption the aquatic plants photosynthesis [5].

Regarding the inevitable industrial needs to the synthetic dyes, the only cure to deal with this kind of contamination, is through using specific ecosystem-consistent mechanisms and methods to recreate a green environment. To this end, researchers have found sustainable methods such as conventional chemical and physical treatment processes like chemical separation and precipitation, coagulation and flocculation, adsorption on activated carbon, reverse osmosis, membrane filtration, desalination, etc. [6]. However, these processes often have special limitations, as they may lead to incomplete degradation and generate the secondary pollutants. Green photocatalyst technology based on advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) offers the advantages over the conventional wastewater treatment techniques [7–9]. This process is based on reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as hydroxyl radicals that are capable of converting organic, persistent chemical pollutants, to simple and non-toxic products [10,11]. This proven and high potential process is very effective and environmentally sustainable [2]. As known, the separation and recycling of nanophotocatalyst (NPC) is essential for reusing, and on the other hand, filtration and centrifugation may cause catalyst loss and consume higher energy. Meanwhile, the immobilization usually decreases the effective catalyst surface, therefore, to overcome the mentioned limitations, the magnetic nanophotocatalysts (MNPCs) have been noticed; they are more effective and can be recycled by an external magnetic field [12].

Up to now, various types of magnetic particles were reported as magnetic core for photocatalytic applications; however, iron oxide (Fe2O3 or Fe3O4) nanoparticles (NPs) were used as suitable cores for preparation of MNPCs due to the lower toxicity and particular magnetic properties [13]. Also, Fe3O4 possesses good conductivity and can act as an electron receptor to prevent recombination of the charge carrier in NPCs [14]. On the other hand, ZnS is a well-known semiconductor with a wide band gap of 3.68 eV and extensive optical transparency from UV to IR [15]. ZnS has also attracted considerable attention due to its prominent optical properties as an important semiconductor. Moreover, compared to other semiconductor materials, ZnS has some advantages such as strong fluorescence, good stability, non-toxicity, abundance and cheapness [16].

Among the green NPCs, graphene has been noticed as an efficient photocatalyst due to its high level of porous structure, chemical stability, high intrinsic mobility, high mechanical strength and high flexibility [17]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be considered as nontoxic alternatives to semiconductors based on traditional QDs [18]. Recently, due to new features and enhancements achieved, the combination of magnetic nanoparticles with GQDs is an attractive topic [19]. It is worth noted that QDs- based photodegradation was applied as promising and precursor strategy by our research group to remove various pollutants such as; methyl violet [20], thymol blue [21], sunset yellow [22], naphthalene [23], malachite green [24] and so. However, utilizing the MNPCs for water treatment objectives is continuously improving. For example, Singh and Sarma used magnetic ZnS as photocatalyst for the degradation of Cr(VI) in wastewater, at pH of 2, under sunlight irradiation [25]. Rashidi prepared and used super-paramagnetic ZnS NPs for medicinal applications [26]. Application of Fe3O4/GQDs nanocomposite was reported by Mohammadrezaei as a magnetic sorbent for preconcentration of bisphenol A, prior to its determination by UV/HPLC [27]. Arshad reported Fe3O4/graphene nanocomposite for removal of methyl orange [28]. Other magnetic QDs have been reported for photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine [29], methylene blue [30] and Indigo [31] dyes.

In this study, by considering the advantages of MNPCs, Fe3O4 decorated with zinc sulfide QDs (named as: Fe3O4@ZnS QDs), and N,S- doped graphene QDs (named as: Fe3O4 @ N, S-GQDs) were synthesized by the sonochemical and hydrothermal methods, respectively. After synthesis and characterization of decorated MNPCs, the photocatalytic potential of the samples was evaluated based on their ability to photodegrade Victoria blue R (VBR) dye as an organic dye pollutant. Due to good magnetic properties of Fe3O4 NPs as well as decorated MNPCs including Fe3O4@ZnS and Fe3O4@GQDs, the samples can easily be collected from the sample solution and reused for further photocatalytic experiments.

All of the chemicals were at highest purity and provided from the Aldrich Company. The solvents and VBR dye (C33H32N3Cl) were purchased from Sigma Company. Double distilled water was used for solution preparation.

Absorption spectra of the samples were obtained using a PG-Instrument UV–vis spectrophotometer (PG Instruments Limited Company, United Kingdom). Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) FEI Quanta200 ESEM (ZFE Graz, Graz, Austria) was used to record the morphologies of the NPCs. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) measurements (4000−400 cm−1) were characterized using a Shimadzu FT-IR 8300-spectrophotometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE (Bruker AXS GmbH, Karlsruhe, Germany). For ultrasonic experiments, a heating system (KMH1-120W6501 model Pulse, Italy) was used. The magnetic characterization of the samples was carried out using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) device made by Magnetic Kavir Kashan Company (Kashan University, Kashan, Iran). All pH measurements were carried out using a ZAGCHIMI pH-meter model PTR-79, (ZAGCHIMI Company, Iran). In magnetic separation experiments, the MNPCs were collected from the suspension by a 1.4 T magnetic field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of unfunctionalized and functionalized Fe3O4 and Fe3O4 decorated with ZnS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: For synthesis the Fe3O4 MNPs, 6.10 g of FeCl3.6H2O (0.02 mol) and 2.35 g of FeCl2. 4H2O (0.01 mol) were dissolved in 100 mL of double distilled water, under nitrogen atmosphere, during 10 min stirring time at 90 °C. Then, 10 mL of NH4OH solution (25 % wt) was added in a drop-wise manner to the mixture and the reaction was allowed to reach to the room temperature. After that, the dark precipitation was separated by a strong external magnetic field. The synthesized precipitate was washed several times with water and ethanol to remove the impurities. The materials were collected and placed in the oven for 12 h, at 60 °C [22].

In addition, ultrasonic assisted approach was applied for synthesis of ZnS QDs decorated Fe3O4 MNPCs at room temperature, according to literature [32], with some modifications (Scheme 1 ). Fe3O4 powder (∼ 0.4 g) was added to the HCl aqueous solution (100 mL; 0.05 mol.L−1) and sonicated for 30 min. Then, the particles were separated using a magnet and washed several times with double distilled water. In the second step, for the synthesis of functionalized Fe3O4 with the anionic surfactant, the Fe3O4 particles were mixed with the aqueous solution of sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) (100 mL; 0.1 mol.L−1). The mixture was exposed again to ultrasonic irradiation for 20 min. In the third step, the functionalized MNPs were separated using a magnet and mixed with the aqueous solution of Zn(NO3)2 (60 mL; 0.1 mol.L−1). The mixture was sonicated for 20 min and then placed in an oil bath, which adjusted at 60 °C. Finally, the aqueous solution of Na2S (60 mL; 0.1 mol.L−1) was added drop-wise to the mixture. The reaction mixture was stirred for 2 h, and the obtained precipitate was separated by a strong magnet and washed several times using water and ethanol and dried at 60 °C in the oven.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N,S-GQDs and Fe3O4 decorated with N,S-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: N and S doped GQDs (N,S-GQDs) were synthesized through the hydrothermal method, as follows (Scheme 2 ): 3 mmol thiourea and 1 mmol citric acid were dissolved in 90 mL of double distilled water, then the solution was slowly stirred to produce a homogeneous solution. The clear solution was kept in a stainless steel autoclave (6 h, 180 °C). The obtained yellow solution, contained GQDs, was collected.

In addition, Fe3O4 MNPs decorated with GQDs (Fe3O4@N,S-GQDs) was synthesized by hydrothermal method, as follows [30]: 1.00 g of Fe3O4 MNPs and 10 mL of the as-synthesized GQDs solution were mixed with 60 mL of double distilled water and the suspension was exposed to ultrasound for 1 h. Then, the homogeneous mixture was transferred to the autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 6 h. Finally, the obtained black precipitate was separated by a magnet and washed several times with double distilled water and ethanol. The resulted magnetic GQDs were dried in oven (60 °C; 10 h) and used in visible -driven photodegradation experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation of VBR using the decorated MNPCs
ce:section.ce:para:
For VBR dye degradation using Fe3O4@ZnS and Fe3O4@N,S-GQDs, 50 mL of dye solution (20 mg.L-1) and certain amount of MNPCs were mixed and the suspension was stirred for 30 min in darkness, then placed under irradiation. A UV-light source (Mercury lamp; 36 W) and a visible-light source (Tungsten lamp, 100 W) were applied for the photodegradation of VBR dye, in the presence of Fe3O4@ZnS and Fe3O4@N,S-GQDs, respectively. The pH adjustment was done using the aqueous solution of HCl and NaOH. The lab-scale photodegradation reactor was equipped with a fan and the distance between the tested solution and light source was fixed at 15 cm, for all of the experiments. In regular intervals, dye degradation was calculated based on the decrease in absorbance intensity of VBR dye at 591 nm, using a spectrophotometer. The degradation efficiency was calculated using the following equation [20]:
D E
%
=
C 0
-
C t
C 0
×
100 =
A 0
-
A t
A 0
× 100

To study the crystalline structure of samples including, Fe3O4, Fe3O4@ SDS, Fe3O4@ ZnS, and Fe3O4@N, S-GQDs, X-Ray diffraction (XRD) was applied (Fig.1 ). The peaks (Fig.1 A) appeared at 2θ of 30.32, 35.67, 43.37, 53.83, 57.25, 62.90 and 74.43 are assigned to the cubic spinel structure of Fe3O4 (JCPDS card No. 19-0629) [33]. Meanwhile, the located peaks at 2θ of 9.41, 17.68, 28.12, 28.66, 33.03, 41.87, 58.61 and 62.77 confirm that the cubic spinel structure of Fe3O4@ ZnS reminded stable after coating with ZnS QDs (Fig.1B) [7,22]. In addition, the known peaks of modified ZnS with crystalline structure located at 28.6, 48.0 and 56.6 can be observed in Fig.1 C (JCPDS card No. 89-2164) [34]. The clear peaks in XRD pattern of the Fe3O4 @ N, S-GQDs sample (Fig. 1D) located at 2θ of 30.49, 35.85, 43.52, 53.92, 57.45, 63.08 and 74.65 are corresponded to cubic spinel structure of Fe3O4 @ N, S-GQDs. These results are in good agreement with previous reports (JCPDS card No. 89-2355) [35]. As reported by Khataee et.al, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of N,S-GQD/Fe3O4 sample indicated the successful doping of N and S atoms as well as attachemnet of N,S-GQDs to Fe3O4 surface [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the as-prepared samples
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption spectra and of Fe3O4 MNPs decorated with ZnS and N,S-GQDs are shown in Fig. 2 (A,C). As reveals, three distinguish absorption bands can be observed for Fe3O4@ZnS around 235, 320, 430 nm and for Fe3O4@N, S-GQDs around 255, 320, 555 nm, which can be attributed to the presence of some functional groups in the surface of MNPCs. Moreover, the direct band gap energy (Eg) of the MNPCs was calculated form absorbance date and the results presented in Fig.S1. The Eg value of the samples from Tauc equation was obtained to be 3.95 and 4.05 eV for Fe3O4@ZnS and Fe3O4@N, S-GQDs MNPCs. According to the previous reports, the observed blue shift in the absorption band compared to macro-crystalline samples was assigned to the phenomena called "quantum size confinement effect" [23]. Thus, the change in the absorption edge is related to the decrease in the size of the particles. In the case of Fe3O4@N, S-GQDs, the absorption bands appear around 320 and 555 nm were assigned to the doping of GQDs with sulfur [36].

FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4@ ZnS and Fe3O4@ N, S-GQDs QDs are shown in Fig.2 (B,D). The UV–vis and FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4 MNPs was also presented in Fig. S2. The distinguished peaks for Fe3O4@ ZnS samples located at 1450 cm−1, 1620 cm−1and 3430 cm-1 have been ascribed to the presence of CO, CO and OH bonds, respectively [37]. Meanwhile, the peaks in 570 cm−1, 937 cm−1, 1623 cm−1, 2923 cm−1 and 3459 cm−1 are respectively related to Fe-O, −COO-, NH, −CH2 and OH functional groups [30,38,39]. Fig. 2 (D) indicates the presence of carboxyl groups on the GQD surface. Meanwhile, after decorating the Fe3O4 MNPs with Fe3O4@ N, S-GQD, the intensity of the OH group has been changed, which shows the main role of this bond in the Fe3O4@N,S-GQD synthesis. After decorating the Fe3O4 nanoparticle, some of the new peaks including CN, N = CS, C–H and = CH has been appeared at about 1500 cm−1, 2350 cm−1, 2850 cm−1, and 3400 cm−1 for Fe3O4@ N, S-GQD sample.

The magnetic properties of pure Fe3O4, Fe3O4@ ZnS, and Fe3O4@ N, S-GQD, were investigated using VSM in an external magnetic field from -15,000–15,000 Oe (Oe) and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . For, ​​ Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@ ZnS samples, the magnetic saturation values of 64.95 and 19.43 emu.g−1, indicate the desirable paramagnetic properties of the as-prepared MNPs. The significant reduction in magnetic property after decorating of Fe3O4, can be as a result of covering the Fe3O4 magnetic core by non-magnetic ZnS shell. The magnetic properties of Fe3O4@N,S-GQDs were studied, too. As seen in Fig. 3, the proper magnetic property of Fe3O4 MNPs can be observed even after bonding with N, S-GQD. Accordingly, the magnetic saturation value ​​for Fe3O4 @ N, S-GQDs was equal to 55.58 emu.g−1, which indicated the desirable paramagnetic properties compared to Fe3O4 NPs (64.95 emu.g−1).

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to study the surface morphology of the synthesized samples. Fig.4 shows the SEM images of Fe3O4, Fe3O4 @ SDS, Fe3O4 @ ZnS, and Fe3O4 @ N, S-GQDs samples. The images show that the mean size of the sample particles is less than 100 nm. The image presented in Fig. 4 shows the spherical Fe3O4 MNPs with the average particle size of 65 nm. However, agglomeration of the particles has been happened partly, which may be due to sample preparation conditions and dispersion. The morphology of Fe3O4 MNPs after decoration with ZnS QDs and N, S-GQDs can be analyzed from the recorded SEM images with different magnifications. As it can be seen, the morphology and mean particle size and size distribution of Fe3O4 @ ZnS, and Fe3O4 @ N, S-GQDs MNPC samples were different compared to Fe3O4, Fe3O4 @ SDS samples. Besides, according to high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images reported by Sun et.al, the as-prepared GQDs were well-dispersed in narrow size distributions (3.10 ± 0.54 nm) with the nature of graphite [36].

After characterization, photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples was investigated based on their performance to remove VBR as a cationic dye from aqueous solution. In this way, the effect of the practical parameters on the dye removal efficiency, including; the amount and type of MNPCs, irradiation time, initial pH of the solution, and the electrolyte concentration on the dye removal efficiency was studied and optimized. The reusability and the kinetics of the reaction was investigated and discussed, too.

The pH of the solution has a significant effect on the efficiency of photocatalytic process. The pH of solution acts as a more determinative parameter and affects the surface charge properties of MNPCs, as well as the size and accumulation of nano-particles. Therefore, the dye degradation efficiency depends intensely on the solution pH value.

In a preliminary study, to evaluate the surface charge of the as-synthesized MNPCs, zero charge point (pzc) was measured in batch experiments. This parameter is a concept related to the adsorption phenomenon and gives the pH when the surface of the sample is neutral. At the pH values lower and higher than the pHpzc, the surface charge of the particle is positive and negative, respectively. To determine pHpzc of the samples, 15 mg of MNPCs including Fe3O4@ZnS, and Fe3O4@N, S-GQDs was added to 10 mL of 0.1 M KNO3 solution, while the pH adjusted to 2–12, individually. After stirring the solutions for 24 h, at room temperature, the final pH value of the solutions was recorded and the difference of the final and the initial pH values was plotted versus initial pH value (Fig. 5 A, B). From the results, pzc values of 4.27 and 6.81 were obtained for Fe3O4@ZnS and Fe3O4@N, S-GQDs MNPCs, respectively.

In addition, the effect of solution pH, on the degradation efficiency was studied over the range of 5–12, in the presence of Fe3O4@ZnS (25 mg) and Fe3O4@N, S-GQDs MNPCs (14 mg), 50 mL of 20 mg.L−1 VBR dye solution. The initial pH adjustment was performed by adding aqueous solutions of HCl and NaOH. The reacting solutions containing Fe3O4@ZnS and Fe3O4@N, S-GQDs MNPCs were placed under UV and Visible light, respectively. The DE (%) was calculated according to equation 2.1 based on the decrease in the absorbance intensity of dye solution at its maximum wavelength. According to Fig. 5 (B,C), the maximum dye degradation was observed at pH 8 and 9, in the presence of Fe3O4@ZnS and Fe3O4@N, S-GQDs MNPCs, respectively. The maximum DE % observed at alkaline pH values can be attributed to the increase in the hydroxyl ion concentration that leads to radical hydroxyl formation. Since, the hydroxyl radicals are considered as the major oxidizing agent in advanced oxidation processes, more beneficial conditions can be achieved for maximum photocatalytic activity, at the alkaline solutions [24,40]. Besides, the electrostatic attraction between the cationic dye species and the negatively charged MNPCs increases the dye adsorption rate of the dye species on the surface of MNPCs, resulted in a positive effect on the photodegradation efficiency. However; at harsh alkaline solutions, the competition of hydroxyl ions for adsorption on the surface of MNPCs may limit the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation. On the other hands, at acidic conditions (pH < pzc), the repulsion forces between the H+ ions and positively charged sites in MNPCs, as well as the cationic dye, leads to a significant decrease in the photocatalytic efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of initial dye concentration and irradiation time
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the initial dye concentration as well as the irradiation time, on the photocatalytic degradation of VBR dyes in the presence of Fe3O4@ZnS QDs and Fe3O4@ N, S-GQDs MNPCs were investigated through the batch experiments. For this purpose, 50 mL of dye solution with certain concentration, in the range of 20−80 mg.L−1, was irradiated during different irradiation times up to 120 min, under optimum conditions. The UV- light driven experiments were done in the presence of 25 mg of Fe3O4@ZnS QDs suspended in dye solution at adjusted pH of 8. For visible-driven photolysis, 14 mg of Fe3O4@ N, S-GQDs was mixed with 50 mL of aqueous solution of VBR at adjusted pH of 9. The results were illustrated in Fig. S3. The decrease in absorbance intensity of VBR dye spectra at its maximum wavelength (591 nm) is quite clear. In a control experiment, the photodegradation performance of Fe3O4 MNPs was examined under optimum condition, the maximum photodegradation of 57.26 % was observed after 120 min UV irradiation (Fig. S4). While, the maximum photodegradation percentage for Fe3O4@ZnS QDs and Fe3O4 @ N, S-GQDs was obtained as 93.44 % and 96.68 %, after 120 min irradiation, respectively.

As obtained, by increasing the initial concentration of VBR dye, the degradation efficiency of Fe3O4@ZnS QDs and Fe3O4 @ N, S-GQDs MNPCs was decreases for, continuously. It may be due to that at high concentration of VBR dye, the number of transmitted photons is decreased, so fewer MNPC can photoactivated; consequently, the dye removal efficiency will decrease [21]. Meanwhile, this observation may be due to low production of hydroxyl radical at high dye concentration [7].

In order to investigate the effect of the MNPCs dosage on the dye removal efficiency, a series of experiments were performed at optimum conditions, in the presence of 10−30 mg and 8−17 mg of Fe3O4@ZnS QDs and Fe3O4@ N, S-GQDs MNPCs, respectively. As obtained (Fig.S5), the dye removal performance of MNPCs, was improved by increasing the dosage used. However, a decline in the photocatalytic efficiency has been appeared at the higher value of MNPCs dosages that can be attributed to the accumulation of colloidal nanoparticles, which leads to a reduction in the active sites number. Meanwhile, at high dosage of the nano-photocatalysts, the permeability of the exposed light through the solution was hindered, thereby; the electron-hole recombination step was disrupted, caused a decrease in photocatalytic activity performance of the samples [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability of Fe3O4@ZnS QDs and Fe3O4@ N, S-GQDs MNPCs
ce:section.ce:para: The possibility of nanophotocatalyst reuse was examined through several experiments: for this purpose, 50 mL of dye solution with the initial concentration of 20 mg.L−1 was prepared and mixed with Fe3O4@ZnS QDs and Fe3O4@ N, S-GQDs MNPCs, irradiated for 120 min, under optimum conditions. After each experiment, the dye concentration was adjusted to the initial value (20 mg.L−1) and the next cycle was examined. The results are shown in Fig. S6. For both MNPCs, the photodegradation performance is desirable until the sixth cycle, after that, the gradual decrease in the next cycles can be observed. This effect can be due to occupation of the catalysts active sites by dye molecules, which leads to a lower photodegradation percentage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of salt, co-existing ions, and enhancers on VBR dye degradation
ce:section.ce:para: In general, there are various salts and metal ions in the wastewater, which may affect the dye degradation efficiency [42]. In photocatalyst assisted oxidation process, the presence of strong electrolytes increases the ionic strength, consequently the activity coefficient of the species is changed. Meanwhile, the adsorption of dye molecules on photocatalyst surface is quite dependent to the production rate of charge carriers or the rate of electron/hole recombination [43]. In this research, to simulate the real industrial wastewater, efficiency of the method was investigated in the presence of different ionic salts (NaCl and KNO3) over a wide concentration range of 0−0.5 mol.L−1, under optimum conditions. As found, by varying the type and concentration of the electrolytes, even at high salt concentration, the photo-degradation efficiency approximately remains constant. As reported, the slight decrease in the degradation performance by increasing the salt concentration may be due to the possible competition of salt ions with the dye species for adsorption on the active sites of MNPCs [44]. In addition, to investigate the effect of various interfering ions on VBR dye degradation, the binary solution containing some foreign ions including NaCN, NaCl, KSCN, K2S2O3, K2C2O4, KIO3, and KIO4 with the salt to VBR dye concentration ratio of 1000 were prepared and the results provided in Fig.6 . As shown, the presence of the selected salts does not show a serious interference in VBR dye degradation process, so the photodegradation efficiency was not changed, significantly. The observed independence of photocatalyst performance from the salt concentration could prompt the applicability of the synthesized MNPCs for the treatment of real samples with high matrix effect.

The effect of presence of two enhancing compounds including potassium persulfate (K2S2O8) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) on the photodegradation efficiency of the as-prepared Fe3O4@ZnS QDs MNPCs was investigated and the results are presented in Fig. 6. As expected, addition of the enhancers of K2S2O8 and H2O2 show a positive effect on photodegradation efficiency. As observed, in the presence of K2S2O8 and H2O2 enhancers, the complete dye degradation can be obtained after 120 min, respectively. As known, the enhancers can improve the photodegradation efficiency of the photocatalytic process by increasing the radical production rate.

Table1 presented a brief comparison between the characteristics of the as-prepared MNPCs used for photodegradation of VBR dye. As it can be seen, Fe3O4@N,S-GQDs shows higher removal efficiency and magnetization compare to Fe3O4@ZnS QDs. Besides, lower energy and dosage of nano-photocatalysts (visible light) were required for photo-induced activation of Fe3O4@N,S-GQDs compared to Fe3O4@ZnS QDs. Also, by comparison the present approach with the previously reported study (Table 2 [3,45–49],), it can be included that the as-synthesized MNPCs can be categorized as the precursor, green, efficient, and reusable nano-photocatalysts even reported.

Today, numerous suggestions have been reported to describe the possible mechanism of NPC-based photodegradation process. However, the main corresponding species involving the AOP were the hydroxyl radicals and the produced electron/hole charge carriers (e−/h+). The formed active species can assisted the degradation of the pollutant molecules and convert them to smaller species with lower toxicity [50,51]. The possible mechanism of VBR photodegradation using the as-prepared MNPCs was shown in Scheme 3 .

In this study, decorated MNPCs including Fe3O4@ZnS QDs and Fe3O4@ N, S-GQDs were synthesized, by using a water-based approach, then applied as green nano-photocatalysts for the photocatalytic removal of the organic VBR dye. The good magnetic properties of the as-synthesized MNPCs provide an easy and quick magnetic separation process, in dye photo-degradation experiments. From the obtained results, both MNPCs indicate the efficient and high-performance photocatalytic activity for the degradation of VBR dye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conflict of interest and authorship conformation form
ce:section.ce:para:
Please check the following as appropriate:
o
All authors have participated in (a) conception and design, or analysis and interpretation of the data; (b) drafting the article or revising it critically for important intellectual content; and (c) approval of the final version.
o
This manuscript has not been submitted to, nor is under review at, another journal or other publishing venue.
o
The authors have no affiliation with any organization with a direct or indirect financial interest in the subject matter discussed in the manuscript
o
The following authors have affiliations with organizations with direct or indirect financial interest in the subject matter discussed in the manuscript:

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: In this research, Fe3O4 MNPs decorate by ZnS and GQDs were synthesized and applied as suitable MNPCs for photodegradation of Victoria blue R (VBR) dye as an organic pollutant. Due to good magnetic properties, Fe3O4 NPs, Fe3O4@ZnS and Fe3O4@GQDs, can be easily collected by an external magnet and reused for further photocatalytic experiments.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2020.112534.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
